channel.title: Nature Communications
channel.description:
Nature Communications is an open access, multidisciplinary journal dedicated to publishing high-quality research in all areas of the biological, health, physical, chemical and Earth sciences. Papers published by the journal aim to represent important advances of significance to specialists within each field.

We are committed to providing an efficient service for both authors and readers. Our team of independent editors make rapid and fair publication decisions. Prompt dissemination of accepted papers to a wide readership and beyond is achieved through a programme of continuous online publication.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Programmable multimodal actuation in cholesteric liquid crystal elastomer hollow fibers beyond mechanochromism
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 27 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-71050-6

Cholesteric liquid crystal elastomers are promising for color-changing soft robotics and photonic devices, though it is difficult to program their thermal or mechanical actuation. Here the authors design a hollow CLCE fiber system with tailored director alignments and preserving structural color.
item.dc:title: Programmable multimodal actuation in cholesteric liquid crystal elastomer hollow fibers beyond mechanochromism
item.dc:creator: Jiazhe Ma
John S. Biggins
Fan Feng
Zhongqiang Yang

item.title: Questioning the near-intrinsic thermal conductivity of suspended graphene membranes fabricated via a cyclododecane-based transfer method
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 27 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70213-9

Questioning the near-intrinsic thermal conductivity of suspended graphene membranes fabricated via a cyclododecane-based transfer method
item.dc:title: Questioning the near-intrinsic thermal conductivity of suspended graphene membranes fabricated via a cyclododecane-based transfer method
item.dc:creator: Mohsen Moazzami Gudarzi
Seyed Hamed Aboutalebi

item.title: Impedance-driven capacitance amplification in dielectric gradient all-fiber non-ionic electronic skin
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 27 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-71173-w

Capacitive sensors based on electronic polarization typically exhibit low sensitivity as their dielectric modulation is confined to a single mode. Here, the authors develop a dielectric-gradient all-fiber non-ionic electronic skin that converts interfacial impedance into signal amplification for tactile sensing.
item.dc:title: Impedance-driven capacitance amplification in dielectric gradient all-fiber non-ionic electronic skin
item.dc:creator: Wendong Li
Le Xi
Mingyang Lu
Jiabao Feng
Siyun Yang
Haoyu Ma
Qinghua Zhou
Miqiu Kong
Guangxian Li
Junlong Yang
